preFix.title: Using refill shops and zero-waste stores effectively
preFix.subtitle: Getting started, what to bring, and how the economics compare to supermarkets
preFix.excerpt: Zero-waste and refill shops let you bring your own containers for dry goods, cleaning products, and personal care items. They eliminate plastic packaging for those products entirely. Understanding what to bring, how pricing works, and which product categories offer the clearest saving makes the switch straightforward.
preFix.sourceNotes: Refill UK app store data 2024. Which? packaging and refill pricing comparison 2023. Wrap UK consumer packaging reduction guidance.

content.text: UK high streets now have over 500 zero-waste or refill shops. Supermarkets have added refill zones in some stores. The basic principle is simple: bring your own clean container. Fill it with the product you want, pay by weight. No single-use packaging leaves the shop with you.
Glass jars, weigh the jar when empty so the shop can subtract the tare weight before pricing. Most shops have scales and will do this for you.
Clean bottles, rinse thoroughly so residue from the previous product does not contaminate the refill. Most shops provide funnels.
Small cloth bags. Most zero-waste shops are happy with washed fabric bags for dry goods if the staff can read the tare weight.

Refill shops price by weight for dry goods and by volume for liquids. The price per unit of product is often similar to a mid-range supermarket brand, not budget own-label. The saving comes from buying exactly the amount you need, from reusing your own container. And from buying premium ingredients at a lower markup than branded equivalents.
Product categories with the clearest saving
Nuts, seeds, and dried fruit, supermarket markup on packaging is highest here. Refill prices are often 20 to 30% lower per gram.
Washing-up liquid and cleaner: buy refill concentrates. These often cost the same as branded bottles and save a plastic bottle each time.
Spices: buying 20 g of a rarely used spice prevents a full jar sitting unused. The refill price per gram is typically much lower than a sealed jar.

Zero-waste shops rarely beat budget supermarket prices on staples like pasta, rice, and oats. Supermarkets use volume buying power to undercut small independents. The reason to buy these in bulk refill is environmental: it cuts out plastic film, outer boxes. And packaging logistics. It does not save money on the weekly shop.
Step-by-step instructions for Using refill shops and zero-waste stores effectively go here.
